data.title: Enphase's Comeback Is Already Underway
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The focus for Enphase Energy (ENPH) has become a recovery story from a brutal reset. The stock has lost nearly 90% from its all-time highs due to declining solar demand, policy issues, and inventory adjustments. Nevertheless, the latest 46% bull run last coverage may be a sign of a possible early-stage correction in the stock price. In the coming years, if residential solar demand starts improving gradually and Enphase continues to deliver on product innovation and cost reduction efforts, the company could regain earnings growth.

Engineering Around Tariffs: How Enphase Turns Innovation Into Margin Power

The rationale for the performance of Enphase stock lies in its tech infrastructure and its product development cycle. This serves as a mechanism for reducing costs and maintaining its gross margin, even with tariff headwinds. Enphase follows a fast product development cycle, as I see it, with its upcoming fifth-gen battery and IQ9 microinverter lines, which will help in cutting costs and delivering a bottomline that exceeds consensus. Enphase Energy is transitioning its battery platform to its fifth generation.

The new hardware utilizes prismatic cells along with a stackable AC-coupled design. It is clear that the design impacts the cost profile for the energy storage business. This generation has a 40% reduced cost profile compared to the fourth generation. This reduction in cost is not a result of any negotiation in the supply chain; rather, it is a result of engineering density. The fifth generation provides 50% higher energy density than the previous generation. This increased density with a reduced form factor helps Enphase Energy stock reduce material volume per kilowatt-hour.

Moreover, this structural cost reduction is a direct countermeasure to tariffs, as Enphase Energy faces a 45% tariff on battery cell packs imported from China. Additionally, a reciprocal tariff of 5% on manufacturing in other regions (like Malaysia and Thailand). The 40% cost reduction through the fifth-gen design neutralizes these duties. It supports Enphase to absorb the tariff impact without shifting the full burden to the consumer and degrading its gross margin level.

GaN Power and Tax Credits: How IQ9 Turns Technology Into Margin Expansion

The IQ9 microinverter series supports this strength, as this product line has Gallium Nitride (GaN) power conversion tech supporting higher power handling (ranging from 427 watts to 548 watts) within a smaller physical unit. With that, I believe the impact of this tech engineering change is two-faced.

First, the reduced form factor lowers the bill of materials and logistics costs/watt, whereas the second is the higher power output that qualifies the product for increased production tax credits under Section 45X. I mean, higher-power products derive more credits per unit sold. Higher-power microinverters garner a production tax credit of ~$0.11/watt. By increasing the wattage density, Enphase sharply increases the subsidy revenue attached to each sale, and this subsidy is a margin buffer.

I mean, in Q4, Enphase Energy disclosed a non-GAAP gross margin of 46.1%. This level is over the high end of the guidance range. Enphase hit this level against a 5.1% negative impact from reciprocal tariffs. This means that the underlying product economics are solid enough to hold against high regulatory costs. The guidance for Q1-FY26 points to GAAP gross margins between 40% and 43% and non-GAAP margins between 42% and 45%, and it is factoring in the ongoing tariff issues.

Further, I think the hardware margin is also supported by software capabilities, mainly the PowerMatch tech. This software feature manages battery output to match real-time home demand. In traditional systems, large inverters suffer from tare loss (energy consumed by the electronics themselves), when operating at low loads.

This approach reduces energy waste by ~40% compared to the use of single large inverters. This efficiency gain improves the value proposition of the battery in the market without adding hardware cost. It increases the usable energy available to the homeowner. By improving the performance-to-cost ratio through software, Enphase also stabilizes pricing power.

Finally, the manufacturing strategy further reinforces this foundation in financials. Enphase Energy ships microinverters from facilities in Texas and South Carolina. These units qualify for the Section 45X production tax credit. Enphase booked an associated receivable of $228 million for shipments made in 2025 and $109 million for 2024. So, I see that domestically produced microinverters enable installers and asset owners to claim the 10% domestic content adder on the Investment Tax Credit (ITC).

This regulatory factor makes Enphase Energy products more attractive to third-party ownership (TPO) providers. TPO partners utilize these credits to finance prepaid lease structures that lower the cost for homeowners. Thus, this factor is stimulating demand for Enphase Energy hardware. The integration of domestic manufacturing with next-gen product specs leads to a cycle where tech density drives tax efficiency that in turn protects gross margins and cash flow.

Balancing the Opportunity: Key Risks Facing Enphase’s Growth Story

The major risk for Enphase's financials is the company's revenue being sensitive to regulatory timing and regional policy issues. This risk leads to issues in revenue recognition and working capital cycles. This risk can lead to friction in cash flow predictability and exposes Enphase to sudden demand troughs when government incentives expire/change. The Q4 pointed to an event as policy deadlines dictate topline patterns.

Q4 revenue came in at $343.3 million, primarily driven by end demand that was pulled forward as US consumers sought to secure the 25D tax credit before its expiration. The increase in revenue likely represents a cannibalization of Q1 demand, which is expected to be in the range of $270-$300 million.

The sequential decline in revenue represents a cyclical trough in demand. The company’s exposure to policy-driven buying cycles contributes to the volatility in revenue. As the company moves towards TPO and prepaid leases, adoption may take longer given the safe harbor rules that are complex and require tax credit eligibility. The 5% reciprocal tariffs on products from Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam also continue to affect margins.

In Q4, these reciprocal tariffs reduced gross margins by 5.1%, and it is a sizable deduction from profitability. Management said on the call that "there is no safe place, which has got no tariff," which I understand to mean that there is a systemic exposure to trade policy that geographic diversification cannot fully set off.

Further, the 45% tariff on battery cell packs from China needs a big supply chain overhaul. Moving to non-China suppliers incurs a cost increase of 20% to 25% for cell packs. This leads to a case where Enphase Energy needs to reduce internal costs just to offset imposed duties instead of using those savings to expand the bottom line more than expected.

Finally, valuation appears to be mixed but improving, with a P/E of 14.5x, significantly lower than its sector median. The forward P/E of 19x could be an attractive entry point for an earnings recovery back towards its estimates of 2027.

Takeaway

The difference for Enphase is not just demand for solar but also leverage. Next-gen batteries, Gallium Nitride microinverters, and tax credit eligibility for domestic production all contribute to lower costs while increasing subsidy capture. This helps to mitigate the effect of the tariff while allowing for gross margins to be sustained at mid-40%.

data.title: FedEx Corporation: Its Valuation Has Already Traveled Quite Too Far
data.description:
Only a month after the publication of my previous coverage, FedEx Corporation (FDX) has already shown some selling pressures and justified my cautious stance. This is not surprising amid intensifying volatility in the market due to the skyrocketing oil prices and stubborn inflation. Its fundamentals may still be robust today, but mounting cost pressures may exacerbate the situation. Additionally, valuation is not that cheap and attractive, which reflects some downside risks. Technicals adhere to it as profit-taking continues due to the recent overbuying.

FedEx Corporation: How Is It Today?

If you remember, I’ve already discussed how FedEx Corporation performed during the second quarter of FY 2026. We all saw the unfavorable market dynamics amid rising cost pressures and softer consumption, exacerbated by new tariffs. Worse, Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) continues to predate on many peers catering to parcel deliveries. Even FDX is no exception.

Even so, its high concentration and strong performance in its domestic market helped sustain its rebound. The Federal Express segment remained the cornerstone as it rose by 8% YoY and covered 87% of the total revenue. More interestingly, its total average daily package volume, or ADV, and revenue per package increased. This shows resilient demand and stronger pricing power. After all, it continues to increase its focus on the B2B market to ensure customer loyalty and secure revenue streams. And for its operating costs and expenses, they increased proportionately to revenues as higher volume meant higher expenses. But with its increased efficiency and lower fuel prices during the quarter, the operating margin still increased.

Middle East Tension

In the past two weeks, protests and civil unrest in Iran have escalated into war after the US and Israel intervened. Iran's retaliation against Iran by attacking many neighboring countries increased the tension in the Middle East. It also blocked the Strait of Hormuz, which supplies about 20% of the global oil market. With that, oil benchmark prices have already skyrocketed. As of this writing, Brent and WTI are $99.48 and $94.79 per barrel. In turn, refined goods like fuel will most likely become more expensive. FedEx may enjoy higher fuel surcharges. But it must also be ready for higher fuel and transportation expenses. Also, this can be inflationary since oil is crucial in running the economy. Its operating costs and expenses may increase and squeeze margins. It must also watch out for the further effect on consumer and business spending, which may affect its volume and pricing.

Another effect is its service suspensions in the Middle East, considering the flight suspensions in countries attacked by Iran, such as Oman, Jordan, Israel, Kuwait, Lebanon, the UAE, and Saudi Arabia. So, its air cargoes/deliveries may be impacted as well and even lead to pickup and delivery suspensions. These things will most likely disrupt its supply chain, which will be costly for FDX. Even a day of suspension or delay. Additionally, it may have to do with rerouting as part of safety measures. Overall, it will increase fuel expenses and war risk surcharges.

Mitigants

FDX is at risk of losing some revenues and incurring more expenses amid the current situation. Yet, it has some strengths to help it survive these challenges.

Its Balance Sheet remains robust. It’s not a risk mitigant in the sense that it will cushion the impact of the Middle East tension. But rather, it can support FDX’s operations even if its revenues and margins drop. Its adequate buffer allows it to sustain its operations and dividends. I’ve already mentioned that it maintains adequate cash reserves of $6.6B, which can cover the main current liabilities, especially accounts payable and the current portion of borrowings. And again, its Net Debt/EBITDA remains low at 1.3x, which means that it theoretically needs only 1.3 years to repay all borrowings. But more importantly, this shows that the business has low leverage.

Its B2B business may support its resilience because this market is more resilient to inflation than B2C. It also helps it avoid exposure to competition with AMZN. Moreover, B2B volumes are typically in bulk and heavy freight. Lastly, transactions may be fixed for a long period. So, FDX may secure and stabilize this revenue stream.

Its heavy investment in AI may also help it at this point. Route optimization amid delays and rerouting may allow its drivers to find the best and most efficient routes or delivery paths based on real-time and data-driven updates. With its SenseAware, it can monitor packages in various locations and geographical conditions. This may even help it avoid risks of parcel damage and losses.

Lastly, it may benefit from the policy easing cycle. This can lower borrowing costs, which can support credit card spending and borrower confidence to partially offset inflationary headwinds. It can also improve its liquidity since it may lower the repricing rate of its borrowings.

Projections And Valuation

A few days before the release of its Q3 earnings, one may be wondering how it is going right now. Perhaps its performance was still well-managed since the tension only intensified during the last week of February, although there might be some dents. But for Q4 2026, I think the impact will be more visible. On a lighter note, its B2B services are typically contracted with tiered pricing based on expected volume. And if you look at Section 8 in its conditions, FDX is allowed to suspend services without liability due to unforeseen/unavoidable circumstances, including wars and civil commotions. In line with this, I decided to forecast its performance for the whole year.

I started by estimating the revenue. Because of the war, I expect its revenue to slow down in Q4 2025, which will also drag down revenue growth for the whole year. To be conservative, I just set its revenue growth high and low at 3.0% and 2.0%, respectively. I think this makes sense if we relate it to the current inflation range. With that, my estimated revenue high and low will be $92.4B and $89.7B, respectively. My estimated revenue high is also very close to the average consensus of $92.74B on Yahoo Finance. Meanwhile, I estimate its COGS (73%) and operating expenses (20%) as a percentage of revenue. I also estimated depreciation and capex using the straight-line method. That way, I may also estimate EBITDA and NOPAT. Either way, it will still be profitable since revenues will still be enough to cover costs and expenses.

In line with this, I also decided to assess its valuation using the DCF Model. I also estimated the operating assets and liabilities by setting them as a percentage of revenue to derive FCFF. Meanwhile, I set its perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to be conservative and consistent with the target inflation. Lastly, I derived WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. As you can see, I set its risk premium at 6.0% to be conservative and price in more risks than the standard of 5.0%. Using all these details, I derived a TP or target price of $308.05. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $292.65. This justifies the selling pressures on the stock. If I lower its risk premium to 5.0%, it will be $354.28. The stock appears reasonable. However, it will still be fully priced, so there is little or no upside potential.

Technicals

FDX's technicals are still generally bullish as the uptrend continues with higher highs and lows. The price also trends above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which suggests that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. However, you can also see the increasing selling pressures. And I believe that is warranted in line with valuation and the Middle East Tension.

Its MACD and RSI also warrant caution as their downtrend continues. The MACD line is trending downwards below the signal line amid the increasing selling volume. Meanwhile, the histogram is within negative territory, which shows that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Lastly, RSI is decreasing as profit-takers remain on the move. The stock became overbought, which justifies a potential dip. Overbuying is also in line with overvaluation. That is why the downward trend is reasonable.

Conclusion

FedEx Corporation is still a well-positioned company with its robust liquidity and strategic focus on the B2B market to strengthen its performance. However, Middle East tensions can disrupt its growth and even squeeze its margins. Valuation is also unappetizing as my target price remains below the current price and suggests some downside risks. Technicals adhere to it as selling pressures due to overbuying emerge. I'm reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: ProShares UltraShort Bloomberg Crude Oil ETF: Still Not For Investors
data.description:
My last article noted that ProShares UltraShort Bloomberg Crude Oil ETF (SCO) was really not for the ordinary investor. The major problem with a leveraged fund (or really leveraged anything) is that you had better be right or the losses really mount up quickly. In this case the Iranian crisis combined with mixed messages from the current United States administration is causing a whole lot of activity involving oil commodity prices. Lots of gyrations are not uncommon due to the mixed messaging. That is not the time to get into a fund that aims to short oil.

Returns

As the fund fact sheet shows, this is definitely not a buy and hold idea.

Note that the fund has done rather well recently (no thanks to the Iranian crisis). Oil prices over the last year have been weak. But to get that benefit, an investor would have had to know when the Iranian issues would begin and more importantly when (and how) that beginning would affect the crude oil markets upon which this ETF is based. Because the Iranian crisis is exactly the opposite of what a fund like this needs.

The other thing to note is that the price of oil has been in a downtrend for three years since 2022. Yet the longer-term returns shown above are negative.

Leverage

This fund is leveraged to produce a 200% return in the opposite direction of oil prices as a general rule.

"This ProShares ETF seeks daily investment results that correspond, before fees and expenses, to -2x the daily performance of its underlying benchmark (the "Daily Target"). While the Fund has a daily investment objective, you may hold Fund shares for longer than one day if you believe it is consistent with your goals and risk tolerance. For any holdlng period other than a day, your return may be higher or lower than the Dally Target."

As the fact sheet notes, the daily investment objective means that the fund itself has got to reset each day to achieve that objective. At the end of this article is the SEC information sheet that goes into even more details on this than I can here.

The problem with leverage is that big changes can happen overnight that are essentially not controllable because you cannot buy or sell. Therefore, holding this after hours given that it moves twice as much as the market can be more excitement than anyone needs.

The expense ratio is 0.95%. That is hardly a consideration for something like this. Actually, the fund itself is administered in a fairly straightforward fashion where the fund is reset to produce another exact duplication of the day before.

This Is Really For Professionals

It might be that professional traders that are very good with charts can use the current situation to make money. But that is very different from the average person.

Furthermore, these things have a tendency to liquidate just before the time comes to make serious money with them. Then the (basically) same type of fund again goes public to start the process all over. Really the entity making money in this situation is the fund advisor.

ETF Weakness

While a fund like this has an accurate daily return. The sum of several daily returns often provides a drift away from the expected result. Some of that can be seen in that the long-term return of the fund is negative despite the fact that the oil price trend is down since 2022.

The reason that this happens is (for example) say the fund reported a 10% loss on the first day. It resets but your principal is now 90%. So if the fund then reports another 10% loss, this time your principal loss is 9% to 81% of the principal left. It is not the same 10% as the day before. So, while the fund reports a 20% loss in two days, your return is negative 19%. That difference will only expand over time.

Latest Return

Since the last article was written, these shares have returned roughly -50%. That is largely due to the Iran crisis escalating oil prices. But a war (or whatever you want to call it) makes it exceedingly hard to tell what is going to happen in the future.

Note that the Iranian situation made for a big loss relatively quickly.

Furthermore, the professionals that are often needed to watch out for these events are often located in companies throughout the industry. These professionals will often do much better over time because they make money not only on the price of oil but also by providing needed services.

Commodity prices are a very low visibility industry. Future prices are very volatile as well. A fund like this could easily fail to return the expected profits because it resets every single day to restore the original structure of the fund. The last article noted this warning in the fund's prospectus.

Safety

I diversify by making baskets rather than by loading up on one idea (let alone using leverage). The issue with leverage is often that you have to get the timing right. Personally, I was never any good at timing the market.

Most of the great investors that I have followed talk about buying bargains and then selling when things are going very well. For oil, many would be selling now as it is highly unlikely that oil will stay over $100 for the long-term.

But many of the great investors sell without worrying about what the stock price does after they sell. Here, that is likely not the case as some serious profits could be missed. Here, timing the market is critical because the profits often happen all at one time. Buying a bargain and waiting for the (in this case) crude oil to move in the right direction can incur serious losses that could more than wipe out the profit opportunity when it happens.

Stocks Instead

Instead, I tend to look for managements where the company grows during both good and bad times. I then have a solid long-term profit without trying to time the market. Instead, I allow the pros at the company to deal with commodities like oil and I evaluate the results. Having an expert at a company working for me has always sounded like a far better deal than trying to do it myself. This puts a pro in charge of a situation rather than a mechanical ETF that I have really no qualifications to invest in.

Furthermore, most of the successful companies I follow in the oil and gas industry are successful without a whole lot of leverage (if any at all). I have a lot of requests to follow leveraged companies in the industry. You can see their tombstones under my profile for all the ones that are gone. Most were leveraged.

EQT (EQT) for example is a natural gas company with a darn good record since the Rice Brothers took over in a proxy battle before the Covid challenges. They have developed a cash generating juggernaut based upon the upstream business of natural gas production and the midstream business of transporting it to market. I would consider holding it until the story changes materially.

Headwater Exploration (CDDRF) is a smaller upstream company that produces heavy oil. It is probably one of the most profitable little upstream companies that I follow. Costs are low enough that it grows in both good and bad times. It is that rare company that pays a dividend while also growing.

Viper Energy (VNOM) this is a royalty company that collects its payments "off the top" with only a few expenses included. The stock price is down due to a secondary offering by selling shareholders that came after a merger. So, there is a fair amount of stock for the market to digest that was either issued for the merger or was sold by selling shareholders. But that will not last forever. The company also has a good record of growing its businesses.

Summary

The problem with any leveraged ETF is that a very clear pathway is needed (in this case down) to even think about an investment like this. Many times, that is not the case. If the timing of the investment is wrong by even a little bit, it can change the profitability of the outcome materially. That makes this ETF a strong sell most of the time.

Secondly, these ETF's often have a liquidation clause as part of the prospectus. Unfortunately for the investor, that liquidation often happens at a time when profitability follows and the fund is no longer around.

In many ways, these leveraged funds are like the lottery where few win. But that few winning often win big enough that many more are willing to lose money trying for that big win.

What usually happens instead is many investors leave the casino when they have no more money.

But that can be avoided by looking for well managed companies that survive the commodity business cycle to overall grow the business. Now an investor has professionals working for him rather than having to be the professional himself. A cyclical growth story can be held throughout the business cycle or purchased during the cyclical downturn while being sold during that cyclical recovery. Now timing is less important. The goal switches to buying a good enough bargain that the sale produces a decent profit (without waiting for the market top). That is a goal within the reach of many investors.

But since market timing is so critical, this ETF remains a strong sell because most investors will not in the long run make money with such a device. It is just better to look for opportunities where the odds are much more in favor of the investor.

Risks

It is possible that at some point the market will have a clear direction either up or down that would provide a profitable trading opportunity. But that possibility is such a rare opportunity, that it is not worth the time for most investors to even think about such a strategy. There are far more probable ways to make money.

It is also very possible that because this ETF resets on a daily basis that any potential investor may have to trade in and out of the fund on a daily basis. It may be particularly important to not have an overnight investment in a fund like this.

These considerations are why investing in a cyclical growth story is far more likely to lead to investment profits.

Here is the link to the SEC bulletin on leveraged ETFs, which you can access for better clarity: Updated Investor Bulletin: Leveraged and Inverse ETFs

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
